title: How the parahippocampal gyrus contextualizes traumatic memories
author: Kalesh
excerpt:
The brain's filing system for fear

metaDescription:
The brain's filing system for fear 

 I spent years convinced my memories were broken. Not the facts, exactly. I could tell you what happened, when it hap...
htmlContent:
The brain's filing system for fear

I spent years convinced my memories were broken. Not the facts, exactly. I could tell you what happened, when it happened, who was there. But the way those memories lived in my body, the way they would hijack my nervous system without warning, that felt like a glitch. Like my brain was misfiring. Like something was wrong with me.

Here's the thing. It wasn't a glitch. It was my parahippocampal gyrus trying to do its job. And failing. Or rather, doing its job too well in one way and not well enough in another.

The parahippocampal gyrus is a small region of your brain, tucked near the hippocampus, that acts like a contextual librarian. It doesn't just store memories. It tags them with context. Where were you. What time of day it was. What sounds were in the background. What you were wearing. Who was nearby. All the sensory and spatial details that help your brain decide if a memory is relevant to your present moment.

When you have a traumatic memory that feels like it's happening right now, even though you know intellectually that it's over, that's your parahippocampal gyrus struggling to do its job. It's lost the context tag. The memory is floating around without its time stamp, its location marker, its "this happened then and not now" label.

Peter Levine, who founded Somatic Experiencing, has spent decades watching this happen in real time with clients. He describes how the brain can store traumatic events without proper contextual encoding. The memory gets locked into the body, into the nervous system, without the brain's usual filing system working correctly. You don't just remember the trauma. You relive it. Because your brain can't tell the difference between remembering and happening.

Stay with me here. This isn't abstract neuroscience. This is your life.

Why your brain keeps treating the past like the present

Think about what happens when you hear a song that was playing during a difficult breakup. Your stomach tightens. Your chest gets heavy. You might feel that old sadness or anger wash over you. That's contextual memory working exactly as designed. The song triggers the context, and the context triggers the feeling.

Now imagine that the song never stops playing. Or that every song reminds you of the breakup. Or that you can't even remember what the breakup was about, you just feel the heaviness constantly. That's what happens with unresolved trauma. The context tag gets corrupted or lost, and the memory becomes context-independent. It can be triggered by anything. Or nothing at all.

Bessel van der Kolk, author of "The Body Keeps the Score," has shown through decades of research that trauma alters how the brain processes context. His neuroimaging studies demonstrate that when people with PTSD recall traumatic events, the prefrontal cortex, which normally helps you distinguish past from present, goes offline. Meanwhile, the amygdala and other threat-detection regions light up like a Christmas tree. The brain is acting as if the trauma is happening right now. Not remembering it. Living it.

Wild, right? Your brain's contextual system, the parahippocampal gyrus and its connected networks, is supposed to protect you from this. It's supposed to say, "Hey, I know this feeling reminds you of that bad thing, but look around. You're safe. You're in your living room. It's 2024. That was 15 years ago." But when the system is compromised, that message never arrives.

For those looking for gentle nervous system support, something worth trying is NOW L-Theanine 200mg (paid link). It's not a fix for the contextual issue itself, but it can help take the edge off the hyperarousal that makes contextual processing harder. When your nervous system is screaming, your brain struggles to access higher-order functions like contextual discrimination. Calming the body first can open a window for the brain to do its work.

The body as a context machine

Here's where it gets even more interesting. Your parahippocampal gyrus doesn't work alone. It's part of a larger network that includes the hippocampus, the entorhinal cortex, and connections to the insula, which processes internal body sensations. Your brain uses body states as contextual cues. How you feel physically becomes part of the context tag for a memory.

Think about that for a second. If your body is chronically tense, if your nervous system is stuck in fight or flight, that body state becomes the default context for everything. New experiences get tagged with old threat responses. Your brain starts to think that the present moment is dangerous, because your body feels the same way it did during the trauma. The parahippocampal gyrus sees the body state and says, "Oh, we're in danger again." Even when you're not.

This is why talk therapy alone often isn't enough for complex trauma. You can intellectually understand that you're safe, but if your body doesn't feel safe, your brain's contextual system will keep overriding your rational thoughts. You can't think your way out of a nervous system that hasn't updated its context tags.

I remember sitting in my therapist's office, telling her I knew my childhood was over. I knew my parents couldn't hurt me anymore. I knew I was an adult with choices and resources. And yet my body was shaking, my throat was tight, my heart was pounding. I was terrified. Of what? Nothing in the room. Everything in the past. My parahippocampal gyrus had not gotten the memo that the context had changed.

A compassionate reframing of trauma that resonates with many is What Happened to You? (paid link) by Oprah Winfrey and Dr. Bruce Perry. It helps normalize why your brain and body respond the way they do, without shame or judgment.

Rewriting the context tags

So how do you fix a broken contextual system? How do you help your parahippocampal gyrus do its job again?

The short answer is that you have to create new context tags. You have to give your brain evidence, over and over, that the present is different from the past. This happens through repeated, embodied experiences of safety. Not through thinking about safety. Through feeling it.

Peter Levine's work on pendulation is one approach. You gently move between the memory of the trauma and the awareness of your present safety. You let the memory arise, but you keep one foot in the present. You notice the sensations in your body, but you also notice that you're breathing, that you're in a room, that you can move, that you can choose to stop. Over time, your brain starts to build a new context tag. "This memory is old. This body is safe now."

Janina Fisher, who integrates trauma theory with structural dissociation, talks about this as "mapping the system." You learn to recognize which parts of you are stuck in the past and which parts are anchored in the present. You don't try to eliminate the traumatic memory. You help it find its proper place in time. You help your parahippocampal gyrus file it correctly.

This takes time. A lot of time. Your brain didn't develop these corrupted context tags overnight, and it won't fix them overnight either. But it can change. Neuroplasticity is real. The brain is constantly updating its maps based on new input. Every time you feel safe in a situation that used to trigger you, your brain is laying down new contextual information. It's learning. Slowly. But it's learning.

If you are curious about inherited trauma patterns, you might find value in It Didn't Start with You (paid link) by Mark Wolynn. It explores how family history can shape the contextual framework your brain uses, sometimes across generations.

The open ending

I don't have a neat conclusion for this. I can't tell you that understanding the parahippocampal gyrus will fix your trauma. It won't. Knowing the name of the brain region that's struggling doesn't automatically make it work better. But it can change how you relate to your experience.

When I feel that old terror rising up for no apparent reason, I sometimes say to myself, "That's just my parahippocampal gyrus being confused. It thinks this is then, but it's now." And that small reframe, that tiny shift in perspective, can create a sliver of space. A moment of choice. A chance to breathe and notice that I'm actually okay.

That sliver of space is where healing happens. Not by erasing the memory. Not by forcing yourself to think positively. But by helping your brain update its context tags, one small experience of safety at a time.

Your parahippocampal gyrus is not broken. It's just working with old information. Give it new data. Give it time. Give it patience. And maybe, eventually, it will learn that the past is over. That you made it through. That you're here now.

A practical guide to working with your nervous system is Anchored (paid link) by Deb Dana. It offers concrete exercises for retraining your brain's contextual responses through polyvagal theory.
